note_data.title: Energy stores and transfers
keyPoints: Magnets have north and south poles - like poles repel, opposite poles attract
Magnetic materials (iron, steel, cobalt, nickel) are attracted to magnets, non-magnetic materials (glass, plastic) are not
Magnetic materials can be magnetised by stroking with magnets, hammering in magnetic fields, or placing in coils with direct current
Temporary magnets lose magnetism when the magnetic field is removed, permanent magnets retain their magnetism
Magnetic field lines show direction (north to south) and strength (closer lines = stronger field)
Electric charge is measured in coulombs and can be positive or negative - opposite charges attract, like charges repel
Conductors allow charge flow through free moving charged particles, insulators impede charge flow
Insulators can be electrostatically charged by friction through electron transfer between materials

definitions.definition: A material or object that produces a magnetic field
A region in which a magnetic pole experiences the force from another magnet or magnetic material
Measured in coulombs and can be positive or negative
Materials which allow electric charge to flow through them because they have charged particles that are free to move
Materials which impede the flow of electric charge because they don't have charged particles that are free to move
Electric charge at rest on the surface of insulators when charged

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show working clearly with correct formula, substitution and unit
Give a sequence of points about what happens

modelAnswer.question: A ball of mass 0.5 kg is dropped from a height of 10 m. Calculate the kinetic energy of the ball just before it hits the ground. (g = 10 m/s²)
modelAnswer.answer: GPE = mgh = 0.5 × 10 × 10 = 50 J. By conservation of energy, all gravitational potential energy converts to kinetic energy. Therefore KE = 50 J
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct use of GPE = mgh
Mark 3: Stating conservation of energy or KE = 50 J

commonMistakes: Thinking magnetic materials are only attracted to one pole of a magnet - they are attracted to both poles
Confusing temporary and permanent magnets - temporary magnets lose magnetism when the field is removed
Not understanding that only electrons move during charging by friction, not positive charges
Assuming magnetic field strength is constant - it's stronger at poles and decreases with distance
examinerTips: Remember magnetic field lines always point from north to south pole and never cross each other
When plotting magnetic fields, use a plotting compass or iron filings to visualise the field pattern accurately
formulas: Efficiency = useful energy output ÷ total energy input
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Sankey diagram showing energy input on left, useful energy output at top right, and wasted energy (usually thermal) at bottom right. Arrow thickness represents amount of energy.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to identify energy stores in a pendulum at different positions and describe energy transfers during its motion
N/D 2022 P1 Q15 (2 marks) — multiple choice question requiring identification of energy stores when a ball is thrown upward at maximum height
M/J 2022 P2 Q2(b) (4 marks) — students had to explain energy transfers when a car brakes, including mention of efficiency and wasteful transfers
N/D 2023 P2 Q3(c) (3 marks) — diagram interpretation showing energy flow in a hydroelectric power station with calculations of efficiency

workedExample.question: A roller coaster car of mass 500 kg starts from rest at the top of a 30 m high hill and reaches a speed of 20 m/s at the bottom. Calculate the energy that has been transferred to the surroundings due to friction. (g = 10 m/s²) [5 marks]
steps: Step 1: Calculate initial gravitational potential energy: GPE = mgh = 500 × 10 × 30 = 150,000 J
Step 2: Calculate final kinetic energy: KE = ½mv² = ½ × 500 × 20² = ½ × 500 × 400 = 100,000 J
Step 3: Apply conservation of energy: Initial GPE = Final KE + Energy lost to friction
Step 4: Rearrange to find energy lost: Energy lost = Initial GPE - Final KE = 150,000 - 100,000 = 50,000 J
workedExample.answer: The energy transferred to the surroundings due to friction is 50,000 J (or 50 kJ). This energy has been transferred from the mechanical energy store of the car to the thermal energy store of the brakes, wheels, and track through heating by friction.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct GPE calculation, 1 mark for correct KE calculation, 1 mark for conservation of energy principle, 1 mark for correct arithmetic, 1 mark for stating energy is transferred to thermal store

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused energy stores with energy transfers' — always distinguish between WHERE energy is stored (gravitational, kinetic, elastic) and HOW it moves between stores (mechanically, electrically, heating)

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to mention that wasted energy ends up in thermal stores of surroundings' — always state that friction and air resistance transfer energy to thermal stores when discussing efficiency

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates often used imprecise language like "energy is lost" instead of "energy is transferred"' — remember energy is always conserved and only transfers between stores, it is never created or destroyed
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw Sankey diagrams showing energy flow. Draw a wide arrow on the left representing total input energy, then split into useful output (continuing straight) and wasted energy (arrow pointing downward). Width of arrows must be proportional to energy amounts. Label input energy type, useful output energy, and wasted energy (usually thermal). Include efficiency calculation: efficiency = useful output ÷ total input.
note_data.quickRecap: Magnets create magnetic fields with north and south poles that interact through attraction and repulsion. Magnetic materials can be magnetised temporarily or permanently, while non-magnetic materials show no magnetic interaction. Electric charges create forces between objects, with conductors allowing charge flow and insulators preventing it unless charged by friction.
